aliases: Grilled Paneer in Spiced Tomato Cream Sauce
Keju India dalam Sos Tomato Berkrim

english: Grilled Paneer in Spiced Tomato Cream Sauce
malay: Keju India dalam Sos Tomato Berkrim

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Paneer Tikka Masala. FOODON_03302051 (cottage cheese food product) is used as the closest parent representing the primary ingredient (paneer/cottage cheese). A more specific curry or masala dish term is not available in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Cheese dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Paneer Tikka Masala or Indian paneer curry code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Paneer Tikka Masala or a sufficiently close Indian paneer curry dish.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, garam masala, chilli, turmeric)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Paneer Tikka Masala is approximately 300 g, comprising grilled paneer cubes in a rich tomato-cream masala sauce, usually served alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Butter Masala / Paneer Tikka Masala — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and USDA FoodData Central reference values for paneer and tomato-cream curry base
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for paneer (cottage cheese) and standard North Indian curry preparations. Paneer Tikka Masala values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN paneer data (per 100 g: energy ~265 kcal, protein ~18.3 g, fat ~20.8 g, carbohydrate ~1.2 g) with a tomato-cream masala sauce component, yielding a composite dish estimate consistent with published literature for similar restaurant-style paneer masala dishes. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Cheese, cottage, creamed' and 'Tomato-based curry sauce' used as secondary cross-check. The spicy restaurant variant adds negligible macro difference. Values represent a typical restaurant-prepared dish at approximately 60% paneer and 40% sauce by weight.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Indian curry dishes and paneer-based preparations — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for Indian curry and dairy-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Paneer Tikka Masala is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 45 (low) based on: (1) paneer (Indian cottage cheese) has a very low GI (~15–27) as a high-protein, high-fat dairy food; (2) tomato-cream masala sauce contributes moderate carbohydrate from tomato (GI ~15) and cream (GI ~0); (3) mixed Indian curry dishes with dairy and tomato base are consistently reported in the low GI range (35–50) in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 18.6) / 100 ≈ 8.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 6.2 × 3 = 18.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~6.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Tikka Masala has a low glycemic index (~45) and a moderate glycemic load per serving (~8.4), making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; pairing with roti or cauliflower rice is preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~5.8 g per 100 g) from paneer and cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from paneer and cream (~17.4 g per 300 g serving) is clinically significant for individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion control to 150 g and requesting reduced-cream preparation can help limit saturated fat intake to within recommended thresholds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Tikka Masala is a popular North Indian vegetarian dish consisting of cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) that are first marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture and then grilled in a tandoor (clay oven) to achieve a characteristic smoky, charred exterior. The grilled paneer is subsequently simmered in a rich, thick sauce made from pureed tomatoes, onions, garlic, ginger, cream, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, and chilli. The dish has a creamy, mildly tangy flavour profile with a robust spice character; the spicy variant intensifies the chilli and pepper notes. Paneer Tikka Masala is nutritionally notable for its high protein content from paneer (~8.5 g per 100 g), significant calcium (~180 mg per 100 g), and moderate fat from cream and paneer (~10.5 g per 100 g, of which ~5.8 g is saturated). Its glycemic index is low (~45), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in appropriate portions. Clinically, the primary concerns are sodium content (relevant for hypertension) and saturated fat (relevant for cardiovascular disease). The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal. It is widely served in North Indian, Punjabi, and Indian restaurant contexts across Singapore and globally, often accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Paneer Tikka Masala?
Is Paneer Tikka Masala good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Paneer Tikka Masala and Paneer Butter Masala?
How much protein in Paneer Tikka Masala?
Is Paneer Tikka Masala high in sodium?
Can I eat Paneer Tikka Masala if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: curry accompaniment with naan or rice

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for paneer and standard North Indian curry preparations, combined with USDA FoodData Central cross-check. No single approved-source entry exists specifically for Paneer Tikka Masala as a composite restaurant dish; values represent a defensible estimate for a typical 60% paneer / 40% tomato-cream sauce composition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database principles applied to the dish components. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (2017) paneer data and standard North Indian curry sauce composition; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. 2. The 'Spicy' and 'Chef Recommendation' qualifiers from the field survey listing are menu descriptors and do not materially alter the macronutrient profile; they are preserved in aliases. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~45, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database principles for paneer and tomato-cream curry dishes; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Tikka Masala is available in the database. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the vegetarian nature of the dish (no meat); individual restaurant preparation should be verified. 7. Serving size of 300 g is a typical North Indian restaurant portion estimate; actual serving may vary by restaurant.
